passages.title: Matthew 16:1
passages.context_html: In Matthew 16, Jesus rebukes those who demand signs, reveals the significance of Peter's confession, and begins preparing the disciples for His suffering and death. The chapter joins discernment, messianic identity, and the necessity of the cross.
passages.common_interpretations_html: ἐπιζητέω (epizēteō (eh-pee-zay-TAY-oh)) — to seek out, search for, or demand. It describes an active pursuit, not a passive noticing. The religious leaders in this story weren't asking for something unreasonable by the world's standards — they wanted proof before they would believe. Jesus doesn't respond by giving them what they asked for. He treats the request itself — "show me something and then I'll believe" — as the problem. That's worth sitting with, because it's the exact opposite of how a lot of popular Christian teaching frames sign-seeking today.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 16:21
passages.context_html: In Matthew 16, Jesus rebukes those who demand signs, reveals the significance of Peter's confession, and begins preparing the disciples for His suffering and death. The chapter joins discernment, messianic identity, and the necessity of the cross.
passages.common_interpretations_html: This is also where Jesus' situation and ours are not quite the same, and the difference is worth naming. Jesus already knew the Father's plan. The cross was not in question — He had said so plainly before this night (Matthew 16:21). And yet knowing the plan did not make the cost any less real to Him as a man. He still did not want to do it. He still said so. And He still surrendered to it completely. If the One who already knew the outcome still found it this hard, and still gave the same answer — "Your will be done" — then how much more should we, who do not know God's plan at all, bring Him our honest fear, our honest grief, our honest "I don't want this," and arrive at the same place He did. That is the real model here. The emphasis is not on whether asking is wrong — Jesus Himself voices what He wants — but on what governs the asking. He names the cost honestly, and in the same breath surrenders the outcome completely. That combination is the model: bring what is true, then release it entirely to the Father's will. There is also something worth noticing in the repetition itself. He asks three times — and Matthew intentionally tells us so. The repetition is not a failure. Each prayer ends in the same place: Your will be done. What the repetition shows is that continued petition is not inherently contrary to surrender. What matters is not whether asking continues, but whether surrender governs it. By the second prayer the wording has already shifted — "if it is possible" becomes "if this cannot pass away unless I drink it." That is a soul working through grief, in real time, in prayer, arriving fully at the Father's will.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 18:15-20
passages.context_html: In Matthew 18, Jesus teaches His disciples about humility, stumbling, restoration, forgiveness, and life together in His kingdom. Its language about agreement, witnesses, and gathered presence belongs to this communal and disciplinary context, not to isolated proof-texting.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Read the full passage — Matthew 18:15-20. Jesus is not teaching on prayer. He is teaching on church discipline — specifically, the process for addressing sin between believers. Verse 15: go to your brother alone. Verse 16: take two or three witnesses. Verse 17: bring it before the church. Verse 18: whatever you bind or loose on earth will be bound or loosed in heaven. Verse 19 follows directly from that sequence — the "agreeing" is the gathered church making a unified judgment in a matter of discipline, not two friends agreeing on a prayer request. The "two or three" language is a direct echo of Deuteronomy 19:15 — "A single witness shall not rise up against a man on account of any iniquity or any sin... on the evidence of two or three witnesses a matter shall be confirmed." Jesus is applying the Old Testament standard for legal testimony to the process of church discipline. He is not establishing a prayer formula. "Where two or three have gathered together in My name, I am there in their midst" is similarly misread. It is not saying that small prayer groups have a special power unavailable to individuals. It is saying that when the church gathers in His name to exercise the authority He has given it — including the difficult work of discipline and restoration — He is present in that process, authorizing and confirming what is done faithfully.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 18:19-20

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "Again I say to you, that if two of you agree on earth about anything that they may ask, it shall be done for them by My Father who is in heaven. For where two or three have gathered together in My name, I am there in their midst."

passages.context_html: In Matthew 18, Jesus teaches His disciples about humility, stumbling, restoration, forgiveness, and life together in His kingdom. Its language about agreement, witnesses, and gathered presence belongs to this communal and disciplinary context, not to isolated proof-texting.
passages.common_interpretations_html: The common assumption is that this is a prayer promise — get two believers to agree on a request, and God is obligated to grant it. The more people agreeing, the more power behind the prayer. This reading has produced entire prayer movements built around "corporate agreement" as a mechanism for guaranteed results. The problem is that this passage is not about prayer at all. It has been completely removed from its context.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 22:32
passages.context_html: Matthew 22:32 should be read within the flow of its chapter and the larger argument of Matthew.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Jesus quotes this exact passage in the Gospels when the Sadducees challenge Him about the resurrection, saying God is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the God of the living (Matthew 22:32). He is quoting from a passage about His own pre-incarnate appearance. Read the full passage in your Bible (Judges 6:11-24). Key verses are quoted below. Again the pattern. The Malak Yahweh appears. The text moves seamlessly between calling him "the Angel of the LORD" and "the LORD" as if they are the same person — because they are. And Gideon's response upon realizing who he has spoken to is the same response every person has: he believes he will die because he has seen God face to face. An ordinary angel would never produce this response.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

deeper_study.title: Who is The Angel of the Lord?

passages.title: Matthew 26:36-46
passages.context_html: In Matthew 26, the passion narrative tightens around betrayal, sorrow, submission, and the approach of the cross. Gethsemane is the setting in which Jesus' prayer reveals both the reality of His suffering and the perfection of His submission to the Father's will.
passages.common_interpretations_html: However, the Psalms were written within the old covenant — before the torn veil, before the indwelling Spirit, before Christ's once-for-all work removed the barrier between God and man. The psalmists cried out across a distance that no longer exists for the believer in Christ. The new covenant changes the terms of access entirely, and the New Testament consistently models prayer from within that already-established relationship rather than reaching across the distance the Psalms describe. Gethsemane — not the petition psalms — is the model Christ Himself gave us for how a new covenant believer approaches the Father (Matthew 26:36-46). Pulling together everything examined above, a consistent pattern emerges, and it is simpler than most people expect. Scripture does not describe prayer as a list of requests we bring to God hoping He grants enough of them. It describes prayer as a relationship in which we submit to Him first and trust Him completely. It begins with His will, not ours. "Your will be done" is not a phrase that gives way to human requests afterward — it governs the entire prayer Jesus gave us (Matthew 6:9-10), from beginning to end. It is asked in keeping with His name and character — "whatever you ask in My name, that I will do" (John 14:13) — not a phrase attached to the end of a request to guarantee the outcome we wanted anyway. It is the asking of someone abiding in Him — "if you abide in Me, and My words abide in you, ask whatever you wish" (John 15:7) — someone living as He would have us live, not someone asking Him to bless a plan we already made. "Your Father knows what you need before you ask Him" (Matthew 6:8) — He is not waiting to be informed or persuaded. Prayer aligned with His will is not a request awaiting a verdict. It is submission itself: "if we ask anything according to His will, He hears us" (1 John 5:14).
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 26:39
passages.note: Gethsemane and surrendered faith: not as I will, but as You will.
passages.context_html: In Matthew 26, the passion narrative tightens around betrayal, sorrow, submission, and the approach of the cross. Gethsemane is the setting in which Jesus' prayer reveals both the reality of His suffering and the perfection of His submission to the Father's will.
passages.common_interpretations_html: 'Not as I will, but as You will.' — Matthew 26:39 The understanding presented here reflects our best study of God's Word at this time. Scripture calls us to come together as the ekklēsia — the called-out assembly — and let all things be done for edification (1 Corinthians 14:26). Your thoughts, questions, and insights are not just welcome — they are part of this process. Iron sharpens iron. If the Word leads us to a deeper or clearer understanding through your contribution, this site will be updated to reflect it. Your voice matters here. This is not a finished work. It is a table we are all sitting at together.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 27:46
passages.context_html: In Matthew 27, Jesus suffers, is crucified, and dies under the weight of redemptive judgment. The chapter's cry from the cross must be read in the larger scriptural and covenantal frame of the passion, not as a denial of the Father's plan.
passages.common_interpretations_html: This is the rawest prayer in the Bible. Jesus is not asking God to stop what is happening. He is not requesting relief. He is bringing the full, unfiltered weight of what He is experiencing into words, directed at the Father, in the darkest moment any human being has ever faced. The Father does not answer immediately. He does not remove the suffering. The cup is drunk to the last drop. If Jesus could bring this kind of honesty before God without it being unfaithful, so can we.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 28:18-20

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "And Jesus came up and spoke to them, saying, 'All authority has been given to Me in heaven and on earth. Go therefore and make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I commanded you; and lo, I am with you always, even to the end of the age.'"

passages.context_html: In Matthew 28, the risen Christ commissions His disciples with all authority in heaven and on earth. The chapter places disciple-making, baptism, teaching, and Christ's abiding presence together at the climax of the Gospel.
passages.common_interpretations_html: The Church's goal is not crowds, comfort, or conversions alone, but lifelong learners of Christ. A disciple is one who walks as Jesus walked — learning His heart, His teachings, His priorities. The early church didn't just preach; they taught daily, walked together, and modeled faith. Evangelism was inseparable from discipleship. Today, the Church must reject superficial Christianity and return to this slow, deep, relational process of growing people into Christ's likeness. Today, however, the mission of discipleship has often been replaced by the metrics of attendance, decisions, and branding. In the Catholic Church, catechism classes are typically tied to sacramental milestones, but often end after confirmation, leaving many with religious knowledge but little personal transformation. Protestant churches may emphasize doctrine or Sunday school programs, yet often treat discipleship as optional rather than essential, reserved for the especially committed rather than every believer. In many non-denominational churches, the emphasis falls on conversion moments or sermon engagement, while the hard work of walking with people — correcting, mentoring, suffering together — is sidelined in favor of weekly inspiration. The result is a shallow Christianity that produces attendees and admirers, but not apprentices. This is not true of every church — many communities practice genuine, relational discipleship — but it describes a tendency widespread enough in modern church culture to name directly. The call to "make disciples" becomes a slogan, not a lifestyle.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 28:19

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "Go therefore and make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit."

passages.context_html: In Matthew 28, the risen Christ commissions His disciples with all authority in heaven and on earth. The chapter places disciple-making, baptism, teaching, and Christ's abiding presence together at the climax of the Gospel.
passages.common_interpretations_html: A textual note: verse 37, in which the eunuch makes a verbal confession of faith before baptism, is absent from the earliest and most reliable manuscripts and is omitted in most modern translations, including this one. The same caution applied to Mark 16:9-20 applies here. The Ethiopian eunuch's response is the NT pattern — immediate. No delay. No deferral. Genuine faith produces immediate obedience. The question "what prevents me?" is the right question for every believer.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 28:19-20
passages.context_html: In Matthew 28, the risen Christ commissions His disciples with all authority in heaven and on earth. The chapter places disciple-making, baptism, teaching, and Christ's abiding presence together at the climax of the Gospel.
passages.common_interpretations_html: The Great Commission is often presented as a program — a call to organized outreach, door-knocking, or evangelistic campaigns. But the Greek grammar tells a different story. Of the four verbs in Matthew 28:19-20, only one is an imperative — a command: mathēteusate, make disciples. The other three — go, baptize, teach — are participles describing how that happens. A more faithful rendering would be: as you go, make disciples. The going is assumed. It is the natural movement of a life already in motion. This means the Great Commission is not primarily a mandate for a method. It is a posture. It is what happens when a genuine disciple — a learner being formed by Christ — lives visibly and openly in the world. Disciple-making is the overflow of discipleship itself. The commission also was never limited to the eleven apostles present that day. Jesus closes with "I am with you always, even to the end of the age" — words that reach across every generation. And Acts 8 confirms it: when persecution scattered the Jerusalem church, it was ordinary believers — not the apostles — who went everywhere preaching the Word.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 2:11
passages.context_html: Matthew 2:11 should be read within the flow of its chapter and the larger argument of Matthew.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Restored reading: "...they fell down and bowed in homage before Him." The Magi were foreign dignitaries following celestial signs — not disciples who knew Jesus as the Son of God. Their act was one of royal homage, recognizing a future king, not religious adoration in the sense of recognizing Him as Savior. This reflects proskuneō in its cultural usage as a status-based gesture, not Christian worship.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 4:10

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "Then Jesus said to him, 'Go, Satan! For it is written, "You shall worship the Lord your God, and serve Him only."'"

passages.context_html: Matthew 4:10 should be read within the flow of its chapter and the larger argument of Matthew.
passages.common_interpretations_html: The second obligation: bow in allegiance (proskuneō) and serve (latreuō) God alone. With Jesus, everything changes. The first obligation — the geographic gathering at a sacred location — is explicitly abolished (John 4:21: "neither in this mountain nor in Jerusalem"). The appointed place is no more. The second obligation — the orientation of the whole life toward God in allegiance and service — is not abolished but deepened and expanded beyond anything the old covenant contained. The believer now bears in their own body the presence that once required a journey to Jerusalem.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 4:9-10
passages.context_html: Matthew 4:9-10 should be read within the flow of its chapter and the larger argument of Matthew.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Restored reading: "...if You fall down and bow in allegiance before me... You shall bow in allegiance to the Lord your God, and serve Him only." Satan's temptation centers on allegiance — recognizing his authority, symbolized by bowing. Jesus rebukes him by reaffirming that such allegiance is due only to God. The use of proskuneō here is a power dynamic, not a call to liturgical worship.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 5:4
passages.context_html: Matthew 5:4 should be read within the flow of its chapter and the larger argument of Matthew.
passages.common_interpretations_html: And there's a sharper example worth sitting with. A Bible study, structured well on paper — an elder opening with real substance, then breaking into smaller groups so everyone had room to participate. Good instincts. But the small group in question drifted into surface-level answers, uncorrected — Matthew 5:4, "Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted," read as mourning over death rather than mourning over sin, with no one present able or willing to correct it. And when the room came back together and that same misreading was voiced out loud to everyone, it went uncorrected there too, even though the elder up front likely knew better. Plurality and participation, without anyone actually steering. That's the dilemma stated plainly: rejecting a titled hierarchy does not mean rejecting qualified teaching. Plural eldership was never meant to mean untrained eldership, or a room where doctrine is simply whatever the group lands on. Scripture's own qualification for the role includes being "able to teach" (1 Timothy 3:2) and able to "rebuke those who contradict" sound teaching (Titus 1:9). A plurality of men who can't or won't do that isn't the New Testament model either — it's just a different kind of drift. What this looks like, done well, is closer to a room of serious students gathered under several capable teachers than either a theater or a leaderless discussion circle. Multiple elders, rotating, teaching and engaging rather than performing. People free to raise real questions, wrestle with the text, and be heard. And someone present, every time, responsible enough and equipped enough to correct the room when it drifts into error — not to dominate the conversation, but to make sure the conversation stays anchored to what the text actually says.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 6:11
passages.context_html: In Matthew 6, part of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus contrasts hypocritical religion with true life before the Father. The chapter moves through giving, prayer, fasting, treasure, anxiety, and kingdom-seeking, so every individual saying must be read inside that unified call to trust the Father and seek His will first.
passages.common_interpretations_html:
Once this connection is seen, the apparent contradiction between Matthew 6:11 and Matthew 6:25-34 dissolves, because both passages turn out to be saying the same thing from two different directions. "Give us this day our daily bread" is not, at its deepest level, a request for groceries — it is the cry of a soul declaring its dependence on Christ Himself as the only true sustenance. "Seek first His kingdom" in verse 33 is the same instruction stated plainly: seek Him, not provision, and everything else will be added as a consequence. The two passages stop competing once "daily bread" is understood as a request for continued life in Christ rather than a request for physical food. This is the language the daily bread petition is actually speaking. It is the prayer form of abiding — a daily, ongoing cry for continued union with the only source of true life, not a request directed at circumstances or material need.

  • Jerome (c. 347-420 AD): Jerome, translating the Gospels into Latin roughly a century and a half after Origen, made an unusual choice. In Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer he translated epiousios (eh-pee-OOH-see-os) with the ordinary Latin word for "daily." But in Matthew's version of the same Greek word, he instead coined a new Latin word that did not previously exist — supersubstantialem, "supersubstantial." Jerome explained his own reasoning directly: he understood this bread as bread "above all other substances, more excellent than all creatures" — and he identified it specifically with the body of the Lord. In other words, Jerome's choice of word was not a translation difficulty he stumbled into by accident. It was a deliberate theological judgment that the bread Jesus had in mind in Matthew 6:11 was not ordinary food at all, but Christ Himself. Like Origen before him, Jerome arrived at the same conclusion this document reaches through John 6 — working from the Greek text itself, more than a thousand years before anyone had reason to argue the point for any other purpose.

Both men were not guessing. Working directly from the Greek centuries before this study began, Origen and Jerome independently reached the same conclusion reached here through John 6 — that "daily bread" was never meant to be a request for food. This reading is not a modern reinterpretation forced onto the text. It predates the tradition that later flattened the verse into a simple request for material provision. Returning to the original language and the earliest readers does not introduce something new into the text — it recovers what was already there before tradition obscured it.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 6:25-34
passages.context_html: In Matthew 6, part of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus contrasts hypocritical religion with true life before the Father. The chapter moves through giving, prayer, fasting, treasure, anxiety, and kingdom-seeking, so every individual saying must be read inside that unified call to trust the Father and seek His will first.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Here is the problem that has to be addressed honestly. Only fifteen verses after modeling a prayer that asks God for "daily bread," Jesus appears to say something very different — that His followers should not even be asking for food, drink, or clothing in the first place, because the Father already knows what they need. Read the full passage in your Bible (Matthew 6:25-34). Key verses are quoted below to walk through the argument. If the two passages are read at face value, side by side, there does seem to be real tension here. One passage models asking God for provision; the other tells us not to ask about provision at all, because the Father already knows and will take care of it. The resolution is not as simple as "pray for it, then stop worrying while you wait" — that reading makes verses 25-34 oddly redundant, since it takes Jesus fifteen verses and an extended illustration about birds and wildflowers to say something that could have been said far more briefly if that were really the only point. There is a meaningful clue in the Greek verb Jesus repeats in this passage.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 6:31-33
passages.context_html: In Matthew 6, part of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus contrasts hypocritical religion with true life before the Father. The chapter moves through giving, prayer, fasting, treasure, anxiety, and kingdom-seeking, so every individual saying must be read inside that unified call to trust the Father and seek His will first.
passages.common_interpretations_html: On the question of daily bread specifically: this document holds that "give us this day our daily bread" is a request for Christ Himself — the living bread of John 6, the fulfillment of the manna typology Jesus explicitly invokes. That is the position argued and defended in Section 4, and supported by Origen and Jerome working independently from the Greek. However, even granting that some may believe daily bread is a reference to physical provision — it does not solve the inherent conflict. Jesus says, fifteen verses later in the same chapter: "Do not worry, saying 'What will we eat?' or 'What will we drink?'... your heavenly Father knows that you need all these things" (Matthew 6:31-33). If Jesus models asking for bread and then immediately says do not seek bread because your Father already knows you need it — the reading as literal bread creates its own problem. The deeper issue is not whether asking is technically permitted. The deeper issue is what genuine faith actually looks like in practice. Tacking "your will be done" onto a list of desired outcomes is not what Jesus modeled in Gethsemane. In Gethsemane the surrender was real. The cup was drunk. The arc of those three prayers is a soul moving through honest expression into complete release — and the release, not the asking, is the model. A person who is already fully there does not need the asking to arrive. Mature faith asks less for circumstances and more for alignment with Him — not because asking is forbidden, but because genuine trust has already released what faith was never meant to hold onto. The New Testament's own center of gravity confirms this: the recorded prayers of Jesus, Paul, and the early church are overwhelmingly aimed at God's purposes, His kingdom, spiritual formation, boldness to serve Him, and the salvation of others. Not because material needs don't exist, but because a life genuinely oriented toward seeking His kingdom first has already placed those needs in the right hands. "Seek first His kingdom and His righteousness, and all these things will be added to you" (Matthew 6:33). The addition is promised. The seeking is the prayer.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 6:33
passages.context_html: In Matthew 6, part of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus contrasts hypocritical religion with true life before the Father. The chapter moves through giving, prayer, fasting, treasure, anxiety, and kingdom-seeking, so every individual saying must be read inside that unified call to trust the Father and seek His will first.
passages.common_interpretations_html: That is the heart of it. Faith does not demand a particular outcome from God, because faith already trusts that He has one. We do not even know how to ask rightly on our own — "we do not know how to pray as we should, but the Spirit Himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words" (Romans 8:26) — and the Spirit's intercession is not according to our wishes but "according to the will of God" (Romans 8:27). If even our asking has to be carried by the Spirit toward God's will rather than our own, then seeking Him, not outcomes from Him, is the only goal prayer was ever meant to have. Provision, healing, and direction, when they come, are what follows when He is sought first — not what is owed to us because we asked correctly: "seek first His kingdom and His righteousness, and all these things will be added to you" (Matthew 6:33). And lastly, the bread we are taught to ask for daily is not bread at all, but Christ Himself, the only sustenance that was ever truly necessary: "I am the bread of life; he who comes to Me will not hunger, and he who believes in Me will never thirst" (John 6:35). "Trust in Yahweh with all your heart and do not lean on your own understanding. In all your ways acknowledge Him, and He will make your paths straight." (Proverbs 3:5-6)
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 6:7
passages.context_html: In Matthew 6, part of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus contrasts hypocritical religion with true life before the Father. The chapter moves through giving, prayer, fasting, treasure, anxiety, and kingdom-seeking, so every individual saying must be read inside that unified call to trust the Father and seek His will first.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Jesus introduces this prayer with an important instruction that is easy to miss: "pray, then, in this way" — not "pray these exact words." He is not handing His disciples a script to recite. He is giving them a structure, a pattern for how a right relationship with God should shape the content and order of prayer. This matters enormously, because the very same chapter, just a few verses earlier, condemns mechanical repetition in prayer (Matthew 6:7) — making it more than a little ironic that this model prayer became, in later church history, the most repeated, recited prayer in all of Christianity. Jesus was not establishing a liturgy. He was teaching His disciples how to think about approaching the Father. The structure of the prayer is not arbitrary, and it does not actually divide into a movement toward God followed by a movement toward us. The whole prayer stays oriented toward God's will from beginning to end. Verses 9 and 10 establish this directly: His name is to be honored, His kingdom is what we long for, and His will — not ours — is what we want done. What follows in verses 11 through 13 is not a shift toward human concerns. It is the same orientation continued: bread that is Christ Himself, forgiveness that restores us to Him, and deliverance from temptation that keeps us walking in His will rather than away from it. Every line of this prayer asks for what God already wills for us — never for something outside it. This means the prayer never actually gives room for personal requests detached from His will. There is no point in the model where Jesus turns from "Your will be done" to "now ask for what you want." A prayer life built around personal concerns is not simply out of order — it is asking for something the prayer itself was never designed to provide.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 6:8
passages.context_html: In Matthew 6, part of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus contrasts hypocritical religion with true life before the Father. The chapter moves through giving, prayer, fasting, treasure, anxiety, and kingdom-seeking, so every individual saying must be read inside that unified call to trust the Father and seek His will first.
passages.common_interpretations_html: It begins with His will, not ours. "Your will be done" is not a phrase that gives way to human requests afterward — it governs the entire prayer Jesus gave us (Matthew 6:9-10), from beginning to end. It is asked in keeping with His name and character — "whatever you ask in My name, that I will do" (John 14:13) — not a phrase attached to the end of a request to guarantee the outcome we wanted anyway. It is the asking of someone abiding in Him — "if you abide in Me, and My words abide in you, ask whatever you wish" (John 15:7) — someone living as He would have us live, not someone asking Him to bless a plan we already made. "Your Father knows what you need before you ask Him" (Matthew 6:8) — He is not waiting to be informed or persuaded. Prayer aligned with His will is not a request awaiting a verdict. It is submission itself: "if we ask anything according to His will, He hears us" (1 John 5:14). Specific requests are not forbidden — Acts 4:29-31 shows the early church asking for something particular and receiving it. What governs whether a request is rightly aimed is not whether it names an outcome, but what that outcome serves. Paul asked twice for his own relief — the thorn removed, a safe and joyful arrival in Rome — and was told no both times, because in both cases he was leaning on his own understanding of what was best rather than trusting the path God had already set. The believers in Acts 4 asked for boldness and for God's power to be displayed, regardless of what happened to them personally, and were granted it immediately. Abraham took the outcome into his own hands rather than asking at all, leaning entirely on his own understanding of how to survive the famine, and it nearly destroyed him. Isaac acknowledged God in the same circumstance, waited on His instruction, and was kept safe. The difference across every example was never how hard or how specifically anyone prayed. It was whether the prayer, and the heart behind it, leaned on its own understanding or trusted the One who already sees the whole path — exactly what Proverbs 3:5-6 calls every believer to: "Trust in Yahweh with all your heart and do not lean on your own understanding. In all your ways acknowledge Him, and He will make your paths straight." That is the heart of it. Faith does not demand a particular outcome from God, because faith already trusts that He has one. We do not even know how to ask rightly on our own — "we do not know how to pray as we should, but the Spirit Himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words" (Romans 8:26) — and the Spirit's intercession is not according to our wishes but "according to the will of God" (Romans 8:27). If even our asking has to be carried by the Spirit toward God's will rather than our own, then seeking Him, not outcomes from Him, is the only goal prayer was ever meant to have. Provision, healing, and direction, when they come, are what follows when He is sought first — not what is owed to us because we asked correctly: "seek first His kingdom and His righteousness, and all these things will be added to you" (Matthew 6:33).
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 6:9-13

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: 'Pray, then, in this way: "Our Father who is in heaven, hallowed be Your name. Your kingdom come. Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And do not lead us into temptation, but deliver us from evil."'

passages.context_html: In Matthew 6, part of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus contrasts hypocritical religion with true life before the Father. The chapter moves through giving, prayer, fasting, treasure, anxiety, and kingdom-seeking, so every individual saying must be read inside that unified call to trust the Father and seek His will first.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Jesus introduces this prayer with an important instruction that is easy to miss: "pray, then, in this way" — not "pray these exact words." He is not handing His disciples a script to recite. He is giving them a structure, a pattern for how a right relationship with God should shape the content and order of prayer. This matters enormously, because the very same chapter, just a few verses earlier, condemns mechanical repetition in prayer (Matthew 6:7) — making it more than a little ironic that this model prayer became, in later church history, the most repeated, recited prayer in all of Christianity. Jesus was not establishing a liturgy. He was teaching His disciples how to think about approaching the Father.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 7:11
passages.context_html: In Matthew 7, Jesus continues the Sermon on the Mount by calling His hearers to discernment, persistence, obedience, and a life built on His words. Promises about asking are framed by the Father's goodness and by the wider demands of kingdom life.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Look at what the passages actually say. Matthew 7:11 — the Father gives what is good, and Luke's parallel names that good thing as the Holy Spirit (Luke 11:13). John 14:13 — whatever you ask in His name, meaning in alignment with His character and purposes. John 15:7 — ask whatever you wish, but only after abiding in Him, which means your desires have already been shaped by His Word and His will. Acts 4 — the early church asks specifically, and what they ask for is boldness to keep preaching regardless of what happens to them personally. In every case, what Scripture models as the content of asking is His kingdom, His purposes, and spiritual formation — not personal earthly outcomes. God wants you to ask. But what He consistently shows His people asking for is what serves His divine purpose. That distinction is everything. It is also worth addressing directly a position that many thoughtful theologians hold: that material asking is not prohibited by Scripture, that Gethsemane itself contains a request, and that the key is not the absence of asking but surrender governing it. Early church writers including Augustine and Cyprian preserved both a literal and spiritual meaning for daily bread. The argument that is often made is that humble petition offered in genuine submission to God's will is valid Christian prayer. This document does not claim that asking is sinful. It does not claim that a person who says "Lord, I need provision, Your will be done" has done something wrong. What it claims is something more precise — and more demanding.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 7:7-11

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: 'Ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock, and it shall be opened to you. For everyone who asks receives, and he who seeks finds, and to him who knocks it shall be opened. Or what man is there among you who, when his son asks for a loaf, will give him a stone? Or if he asks for a fish, he will not give him a snake, will he? If you then, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in heaven give what is good to those who ask Him!'

passages.context_html: In Matthew 7, Jesus continues the Sermon on the Mount by calling His hearers to discernment, persistence, obedience, and a life built on His words. Promises about asking are framed by the Father's goodness and by the wider demands of kingdom life.
passages.common_interpretations_html: Verses 7-8 are the part people usually remember — ask and it will be given. But verse 11 defines the terms of the promise: the Father gives what is good, not necessarily what was specifically requested. "Good" is determined by the Father's judgment, not the asker's preference. A child may ask for something harmful, dressed up as a need. The Father's goodness is precisely what filters the request. Luke's version is even more pointed. Where Matthew has "good things," Luke specifies the gift as the Holy Spirit. This tells us what Jesus considered the ultimate good gift to be — not material provision or circumstantial change, but the Spirit Himself. The two accounts together suggest that the asking and giving Jesus describes here is ultimately about spiritual gifts, not a blank check for personal requests.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 8:2
passages.context_html: Matthew 8:2 should be read within the flow of its chapter and the larger argument of Matthew.
passages.common_interpretations_html: The leper's act was not ceremonial worship but a deeply personal plea expressed through physical humility — bowing in recognition of divine authority. His posture reflects proskuneō as reverent submission, not religious ritual. Restored reading: "...he bowed in reverence before Him."
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Matthew 9:20-22

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "And behold, a woman who had been suffering from a hemorrhage for twelve years, came up behind Him and touched the fringe of His cloak; for she was saying to herself, 'If I only touch His garment, I will get well.' But Jesus turning and seeing her said, 'Daughter, take courage; your faith has healed you.' And at once the woman was healed."

passages.context_html: In Matthew 9, Jesus' healing ministry reveals His authority to forgive, restore, and call sinners. Acts of healing in the chapter are repeatedly tied to faith and to the arrival of the kingdom in His person.
passages.common_interpretations_html: These two accounts are not identical, and the difference is worth naming rather than smoothing over. The woman never spoke a word to Jesus. She reasoned within herself, reached out, and touched His garment — pure faith, with no request voiced at all. The blind man's case is different: Jesus directly asked him, "What do you want Me to do for you?" and he answered specifically, "I want to regain my sight." Here a want was actually spoken aloud, in response to Christ's own question. But notice what Jesus does not say in either case. He does not say "because you asked correctly" or "because you asked at all." In both accounts — the one where nothing was asked, and the one where Jesus Himself drew out the request — His verdict is the same: "your faith has made you well." The presence or absence of a spoken request changes nothing about what Jesus credits. Faith is what He names both times. This is consistent with Hebrews 11:6 — what pleases God is seeking Him, not the words used in seeking Him.
passages.current_position_html: No defined position is stated here yet beyond the common interpretations above. As related study develops, this section will be updated where the text warrants a clearer conclusion.

passages.title: Mark 10:51-52

versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "And Jesus said to him, 'What do you want Me to do for you?' And the blind man said to Him, 'Rabboni, I want to regain my sight!' And Jesus said to him, 'Go; your faith has made you well.' Immediately he regained his sight and began following Him on the road."

passages.context_html: In Mark 10, Jesus is teaching about discipleship, dependence, and the reversal of worldly expectations on the road to Jerusalem. Individual healings in the chapter are tied to faith, sight, and following Jesus in the way of the cross.
passages.common_interpretations_html: These two accounts are not identical, and the difference is worth naming rather than smoothing over. The woman never spoke a word to Jesus. She reasoned within herself, reached out, and touched His garment — pure faith, with no request voiced at all.
